question:
Find all real polynomials $p(x)$ of degree $n \geq 2$ for which there
exist real numbers $r_1 < r_2 < \cdots < r_n$ such that
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $p(r_i) = 0, \qquad i = 1, 2, \dots, n,$ and
    \item $p' \left( \frac{r_i + r_{i+1}}{2} \right) = 0 \qquad i = 1, 2,
    \dots, n-1,$
\end{enumerate}
where $p'(x)$ denotes the derivative of $p(x)$.
solution:
Solution.
All degree 2 polynomials with 2 distinct real zeros work. If \( p(x)=a x^{2}+b x+c \) has two real zeros \( r_{1}<r_{2} \) (i.e., \( b^{2}-4 a c>0 \) ), then
\[
p(x)=a\left(x-r_{1}\right)\left(x-r_{2}\right) ;
\]
comparing coefficients of \( x \), we get \( -b / a=r_{1}+r_{2} \), from which
\[
p^{\prime}(x)=2 a\left(x-\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) / 2\right) .
\]

Geometrically, this is clearer: \( y=p(x) \) is a parabola, symmetric about some vertical axis \( x=d \), and \( p^{\prime}(d)=0 \). The zeros \( x=r_{1}, x=r_{2} \) must also be symmetric about the axis, so \( d=\left(r_{1}+r_{2}\right) / 2 \).

No polynomial of higher degree works. Suppose \( r_{1}<\cdots<r_{n} \) are all real and \( n>2 \), so
\[
p(x)=a\left(x-r_{1}\right)\left(x-r_{2}\right) \cdots\left(x-r_{n}\right) .
\]

Let \( r=\left(r_{n-1}+r_{n}\right) / 2 \).
From here, we can follow two (similar) approaches.
Approach 1 uses the following exercise: If \( p(x) \) is a degree \( n \) polynomial with zeros \( r_{1}, \ldots, r_{n} \), then
\[
p^{\prime}(x)=p(x)\left(\frac{1}{x-r_{1}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{x-r_{n}}\right)
\]
for \( x \notin\left\{r_{1}, \ldots, r_{n}\right\} \). (This can be shown using the product rule for derivatives, or more directly by computing \( \frac{d}{d x} \ln p(x) \) in two ways. This useful equation also comes up in 1992A2.) Since \( p(r) \neq 0 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{p^{\prime}(r)}{p(r)} & =\frac{1}{r-r_{1}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{r-r_{n-2}}+\frac{1}{r-r_{n-1}}+\frac{1}{r-r_{n}} \\
& =\frac{1}{r-r_{1}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{r-r_{n-2}} \quad\left(\text { since } r-r_{n}=-\left(r-r_{n-1}\right)\right) \\
& >0,
\end{aligned}
\]
so \( p^{\prime}(r) \neq 0 \).
Approach 2 is the following: let \( q(x)=\left(x-r_{1}\right) \cdots\left(x-r_{n-2}\right) \) so
\[
p(x)=\left(x-r_{1}\right)\left(x-r_{2}\right) \cdot q(x),
\]
and apply the product rule to obtain
\[
p^{\prime}(x)=a \cdot 2(x-r) q(x)+a\left(x-r_{n-1}\right)\left(x-r_{n}\right) q^{\prime}(x) .
\]

Rolle's Theorem (see remark below) implies that all the zeros of \( q^{\prime}(x) \) lie between \( r_{1} \) and \( r_{n-2} \). Hence \( \left(r_{n-1}+r_{n}\right) / 2 \) is not a zero of \( q^{\prime}(x) \), so \( p(x) \) does not satisfy the hypotheses of the problem.

descriptive_long.question:
Find all real polynomials $polyfunc(varinput)$ of degree $degreeint \geq 2$ for which there
exist real numbers $rootfirst < rootsecond < \cdots < rootfinal$ such that
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $polyfunc(rootiter) = 0, \qquad indexiter = 1, 2, \dots, degreeint,$ and
    \item $polyfunc' \left( \frac{rootiter + rootnext}{2} \right) = 0 \qquad indexiter = 1, 2,
    \dots, degreeint-1,$
\end{enumerate}
where $polyfunc'(varinput)$ denotes the derivative of $polyfunc(varinput)$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution.
All degree 2 polynomials with 2 distinct real zeros work. If \( polyfunc(varinput)=coefalpha varinput^{2}+coefbravo varinput+coefcharlie \) has two real zeros \( rootfirst<rootsecond \) (i.e., \( coefbravo^{2}-4 coefalpha coefcharlie>0 \) ), then
\[
polyfunc(varinput)=coefalpha\left(varinput-rootfirst\right)\left(varinput-rootsecond\right) ;
\]
comparing coefficients of \( varinput \), we get \( -coefbravo / coefalpha=rootfirst+rootsecond \), from which
\[
polyfunc^{\prime}(varinput)=2 coefalpha\left(varinput-\left(rootfirst+rootsecond\right) / 2\right) .
\]

Geometrically, this is clearer: \( ordinate=polyfunc(varinput) \) is a parabola, symmetric about some vertical axis \( varinput=axispoint \), and \( polyfunc^{\prime}(axispoint)=0 \). The zeros \( varinput=rootfirst, varinput=rootsecond \) must also be symmetric about the axis, so \( axispoint=\left(rootfirst+rootsecond\right) / 2 \).

No polynomial of higher degree works. Suppose \( rootfirst<\cdots<rootfinal \) are all real and \( degreeint>2 \), so
\[
polyfunc(varinput)=coefalpha\left(varinput-rootfirst\right)\left(varinput-rootsecond\right) \cdots\left(varinput-rootfinal\right) .
\]

Let \( medianroot=\left(rootprev+rootfinal\right) / 2 \).
From here, we can follow two (similar) approaches.
Approach 1 uses the following exercise: If \( polyfunc(varinput) \) is a degree \( degreeint \) polynomial with zeros \( rootfirst, \ldots, rootfinal \), then
\[
polyfunc^{\prime}(varinput)=polyfunc(varinput)\left(\frac{1}{varinput-rootfirst}+\cdots+\frac{1}{varinput-rootfinal}\right)
\]
for \( varinput \notin\left\{rootfirst, \ldots, rootfinal\right\} \). (This can be shown using the product rule for derivatives, or more directly by computing \( \frac{axispoint}{axispoint varinput} \ln polyfunc(varinput) \) in two ways. This useful equation also comes up in 1992A2.) Since \( polyfunc(medianroot) \neq 0 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{polyfunc^{\prime}(medianroot)}{polyfunc(medianroot)} & =\frac{1}{medianroot-rootfirst}+\cdots+\frac{1}{medianroot-rootpreprev}+\frac{1}{medianroot-rootprev}+\frac{1}{medianroot-rootfinal} \\
& =\frac{1}{medianroot-rootfirst}+\cdots+\frac{1}{medianroot-rootpreprev} \quad\left(\text { since } medianroot-rootfinal=-\left(medianroot-rootprev\right)\right) \\
& >0,
\end{aligned}
\]
so \( polyfunc^{\prime}(medianroot) \neq 0 \).

Approach 2 is the following: let \( auxipoly(varinput)=\left(varinput-rootfirst\right) \cdots\left(varinput-rootpreprev\right) \) so
\[
polyfunc(varinput)=\left(varinput-rootfirst\right)\left(varinput-rootsecond\right) \cdot auxipoly(varinput),
\]
and apply the product rule to obtain
\[
polyfunc^{\prime}(varinput)=coefalpha \cdot 2(varinput-medianroot) auxipoly(varinput)+coefalpha\left(varinput-rootprev\right)\left(varinput-rootfinal\right) auxipoly^{\prime}(varinput) .
\]

Rolle's Theorem (see remark below) implies that all the zeros of \( auxipoly^{\prime}(varinput) \) lie between \( rootfirst \) and \( rootpreprev \). Hence \( \left(rootprev+rootfinal\right) / 2 \) is not a zero of \( auxipoly^{\prime}(varinput) \), so \( polyfunc(varinput) \) does not satisfy the hypotheses of the problem.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Find all real polynomials $waterfall(galaxies)$ of degree $honeycomb \geq 2$ for which there
exist real numbers $pinecone < drumline < \cdots < snowflake$ such that
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $waterfall(firewood) = 0, \qquad lanterns = 1, 2, \dots, honeycomb,$ and
    \item $waterfall' \left( \frac{firewood + gemstone}{2} \right) = 0 \qquad lanterns = 1, 2,
    \dots, honeycomb-1,$
\end{enumerate}
where $waterfall'(galaxies)$ denotes the derivative of $waterfall(galaxies)$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution.
All degree 2 polynomials with 2 distinct real zeros work. If \( waterfall(galaxies)=riverbank galaxies^{2}+starfruit galaxies+cloudship \) has two real zeros \( pinecone<drumline \) (i.e., \( starfruit^{2}-4 riverbank cloudship>0 \) ), then
\[
waterfall(galaxies)=riverbank\left(galaxies-pinecone\right)\left(galaxies-drumline\right) ;
\]
comparing coefficients of \( galaxies \), we get \( -starfruit / riverbank=pinecone+drumline \), from which
\[
waterfall^{\prime}(galaxies)=2 riverbank\left(galaxies-\left(pinecone+drumline\right) / 2\right) .
\]

Geometrically, this is clearer: \( arrowhead=waterfall(galaxies) \) is a parabola, symmetric about some vertical axis \( galaxies=moonbeam \), and \( waterfall^{\prime}(moonbeam)=0 \). The zeros \( galaxies=pinecone, galaxies=drumline \) must also be symmetric about the axis, so \( moonbeam=\left(pinecone+drumline\right) / 2 \).

No polynomial of higher degree works. Suppose \( pinecone<\cdots<snowflake \) are all real and \( honeycomb>2 \), so
\[
waterfall(galaxies)=riverbank\left(galaxies-pinecone\right)\left(galaxies-drumline\right) \cdots\left(galaxies-snowflake\right) .
\]

Let \( sailboat=\left(paintbox+snowflake\right) / 2 \).
From here, we can follow two (similar) approaches.
Approach 1 uses the following exercise: If \( waterfall(galaxies) \) is a degree \( honeycomb \) polynomial with zeros \( pinecone, \ldots, snowflake \), then
\[
waterfall^{\prime}(galaxies)=waterfall(galaxies)\left(\frac{1}{galaxies-pinecone}+\cdots+\frac{1}{galaxies-snowflake}\right)
\]
for \( galaxies \notin\left\{pinecone, \ldots, snowflake\right\} \). (This can be shown using the product rule for derivatives, or more directly by computing \( \frac{moonbeam}{moonbeam galaxies} \ln waterfall(galaxies) \) in two ways. This useful equation also comes up in 1992A2.) Since \( waterfall(sailboat) \neq 0 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{waterfall^{\prime}(sailboat)}{waterfall(sailboat)} & =\frac{1}{sailboat-pinecone}+\cdots+\frac{1}{sailboat-clockface}+\frac{1}{sailboat-paintbox}+\frac{1}{sailboat-snowflake} \\
& =\frac{1}{sailboat-pinecone}+\cdots+\frac{1}{sailboat-clockface} \quad\left(\text { since } sailboat-snowflake=-\left(sailboat-paintbox\right)\right) \\
& >0,
\end{aligned}
\]
so \( waterfall^{\prime}(sailboat) \neq 0 \).
Approach 2 is the following: let \( sandstorm(galaxies)=\left(galaxies-pinecone\right) \cdots\left(galaxies-clockface\right) \) so
\[
waterfall(galaxies)=\left(galaxies-pinecone\right)\left(galaxies-drumline\right) \cdot sandstorm(galaxies),
\]
and apply the product rule to obtain
\[
waterfall^{\prime}(galaxies)=riverbank \cdot 2(galaxies-sailboat) sandstorm(galaxies)+riverbank\left(galaxies-paintbox\right)\left(galaxies-snowflake\right) sandstorm^{\prime}(galaxies) .
\]

Rolle's Theorem (see remark below) implies that all the zeros of \( sandstorm^{\prime}(galaxies) \) lie between \( pinecone \) and \( clockface \). Hence \( \left(paintbox+snowflake\right) / 2 \) is not a zero of \( sandstorm^{\prime}(galaxies) \), so \( waterfall(galaxies) \) does not satisfy the hypotheses of the problem.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Find all real polynomials $antipolynomial(fixedvalue)$ of degree $nodegree \geq 2$ for which there
exist real numbers $summitone < summittwo < \cdots < summitlast$ such that
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $antipolynomial(summitindex) = 0, \qquad aggregate = 1, 2, \dots, nodegree,$ and
    \item $antipolynomial' \left( \frac{summitindex + summitnext}{2} \right) = 0 \qquad aggregate = 1, 2,
    \dots, nodegree-1,$
\end{enumerate}
where $antipolynomial'(fixedvalue)$ denotes the derivative of $antipolynomial(fixedvalue)$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution.
All degree 2 polynomials with 2 distinct real zeros work. If \( antipolynomial(fixedvalue)=trailercoeff fixedvalue^{2}+outercoeff fixedvalue+variableterm \) has two real zeros \( summitone<summittwo \) (i.e., \( outercoeff^{2}-4 trailercoeff variableterm>0 \) ), then
\[
antipolynomial(fixedvalue)=trailercoeff\left(fixedvalue-summitone\right)\left(fixedvalue-summittwo\right) ;
\]
comparing coefficients of \( fixedvalue \), we get \( -outercoeff / trailercoeff=summitone+summittwo \), from which
\[
antipolynomial^{\prime}(fixedvalue)=2 trailercoeff\left(fixedvalue-\left(summitone+summittwo\right) / 2\right) .
\]

Geometrically, this is clearer: \( horizontal=antipolynomial(fixedvalue) \) is a parabola, symmetric about some vertical axis \( fixedvalue=skewline \), and \( antipolynomial^{\prime}(skewline)=0 \). The zeros \( fixedvalue=summitone, fixedvalue=summittwo \) must also be symmetric about the axis, so \( skewline=\left(summitone+summittwo\right) / 2 \).

No polynomial of higher degree works. Suppose \( summitone<\cdots<summitlast \) are all real and \( nodegree>2 \), so
\[
antipolynomial(fixedvalue)=trailercoeff\left(fixedvalue-summitone\right)\left(fixedvalue-summittwo\right) \cdots\left(fixedvalue-summitlast\right) .
\]

Let \( peakpoint=\left(summitprev+ summitlast\right) / 2 \).
From here, we can follow two (similar) approaches.
Approach 1 uses the following exercise: If \( antipolynomial(fixedvalue) \) is a degree \( nodegree \) polynomial with zeros \( summitone, \ldots, summitlast \), then
\[
antipolynomial^{\prime}(fixedvalue)=antipolynomial(fixedvalue)\left(\frac{1}{fixedvalue-summitone}+\cdots+\frac{1}{fixedvalue-summitlast}\right)
\]
for \( fixedvalue \notin\left\{summitone, \ldots, summitlast\right\} \). (This can be shown using the product rule for derivatives, or more directly by computing \( \frac{d}{d fixedvalue} \ln antipolynomial(fixedvalue) \) in two ways. This useful equation also comes up in 1992A2.) Since \( antipolynomial(peakpoint) \neq 0 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{antipolynomial^{\prime}(peakpoint)}{antipolynomial(peakpoint)} & =\frac{1}{peakpoint-summitone}+\cdots+\frac{1}{peakpoint-summitpreprev}+\frac{1}{peakpoint-summitprev}+\frac{1}{peakpoint-summitlast} \\
& =\frac{1}{peakpoint-summitone}+\cdots+\frac{1}{peakpoint-summitpreprev} \quad\left(\text { since } peakpoint-summitlast=-\left(peakpoint-summitprev\right)\right) \\
& >0,
\end{aligned}
\]
so \( antipolynomial^{\prime}(peakpoint) \neq 0 \).
Approach 2 is the following: let \( antiproduct(fixedvalue)=\left(fixedvalue-summitone\right) \cdots\left(fixedvalue-summitpreprev\right) \) so
\[
antipolynomial(fixedvalue)=\left(fixedvalue-summitone\right)\left(fixedvalue-summittwo\right) \cdot antiproduct(fixedvalue),
\]
and apply the product rule to obtain
\[
antipolynomial^{\prime}(fixedvalue)=trailercoeff \cdot 2(fixedvalue-peakpoint) antiproduct(fixedvalue)+trailercoeff\left(fixedvalue-summitprev\right)\left(fixedvalue-summitlast\right) antiproduct^{\prime}(fixedvalue) .
\]

Rolle's Theorem (see remark below) implies that all the zeros of \( antiproduct^{\prime}(fixedvalue) \) lie between \( summitone \) and \( summitpreprev \). Hence \( \left(summitprev+ summitlast\right) / 2 \) is not a zero of \( antiproduct^{\prime}(fixedvalue) \), so \( antipolynomial(fixedvalue) \) does not satisfy the hypotheses of the problem.

garbled_string.question:
Find all real polynomials $lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq)$ of degree $spqwzodr \geq 2$ for which there exist real numbers $mkdlsaeu < snvgrtpo < \cdots < cqhufyal$ such that
\begin{enumerate}
    \item $lkvmdsazo(jplxrfew) = 0, \qquad hrfqeujb = 1, 2, \dots, spqwzodr,$ and
    \item $lkvmdsazo' \left( \frac{jplxrfew + bsdmhkij}{2} \right) = 0 \qquad hrfqeujb = 1, 2,
    \dots, spqwzodr-1,$
\end{enumerate}
where $lkvmdsazo'(ztgfwhnq)$ denotes the derivative of $lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq)$.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution.
All degree 2 polynomials with 2 distinct real zeros work. If \( lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq)=nmhegqiv \, ztgfwhnq^{2}+rzukcdal \, ztgfwhnq+yjtfmsep \) has two real zeros \( mkdlsaeu<snvgrtpo \) (i.e., \( rzukcdal^{2}-4 nmhegqiv yjtfmsep>0 \) ), then
\[
lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq)=nmhegqiv\left(ztgfwhnq-mkdlsaeu\right)\left(ztgfwhnq-snvgrtpo\right) ;
\]
comparing coefficients of \( ztgfwhnq \), we get \( -rzukcdal / nmhegqiv=mkdlsaeu+snvgrtpo \), from which
\[
lkvmdsazo^{\prime}(ztgfwhnq)=2 nmhegqiv\left(ztgfwhnq-\left(mkdlsaeu+snvgrtpo\right) / 2\right) .
\]

Geometrically, this is clearer: \( pvneqair=lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq) \) is a parabola, symmetric about some vertical axis \( ztgfwhnq=urqhsmnt \), and \( lkvmdsazo^{\prime}(urqhsmnt)=0 \). The zeros \( ztgfwhnq=mkdlsaeu, ztgfwhnq=snvgrtpo \) must also be symmetric about the axis, so \( urqhsmnt=\left(mkdlsaeu+snvgrtpo\right) / 2 \).

No polynomial of higher degree works. Suppose \( mkdlsaeu<\cdots<cqhufyal \) are all real and \( spqwzodr>2 \), so
\[
lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq)=nmhegqiv\left(ztgfwhnq-mkdlsaeu\right)\left(ztgfwhnq-snvgrtpo\right) \cdots\left(ztgfwhnq-cqhufyal\right) .
\]

Let \( oqzjfhxk=\left(vtaxogre+cqhufyal\right) / 2 \).
From here, we can follow two (similar) approaches.
Approach 1 uses the following exercise: If \( lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq) \) is a degree \( spqwzodr \) polynomial with zeros \( mkdlsaeu, \ldots, cqhufyal \), then
\[
lkvmdsazo^{\prime}(ztgfwhnq)=lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq)\left(\frac{1}{ztgfwhnq-mkdlsaeu}+\cdots+\frac{1}{ztgfwhnq-cqhufyal}\right)
\]
for \( ztgfwhnq \notin\left\{mkdlsaeu, \ldots, cqhufyal\right\} \). (This can be shown using the product rule for derivatives, or more directly by computing \( \frac{d}{d ztgfwhnq} \\ln lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq) \) in two ways. This useful equation also comes up in 1992A2.) Since \( lkvmdsazo(oqzjfhxk) \neq 0 \),
\[
\begin{aligned}
\frac{lkvmdsazo^{\prime}(oqzjfhxk)}{lkvmdsazo(oqzjfhxk)} & =\frac{1}{oqzjfhxk-mkdlsaeu}+\cdots+\frac{1}{oqzjfhxk-wipjskel}+\frac{1}{oqzjfhxk-vtaxogre}+\frac{1}{oqzjfhxk-cqhufyal} \\
& =\frac{1}{oqzjfhxk-mkdlsaeu}+\cdots+\frac{1}{oqzjfhxk-wipjskel} \\quad\left(\text { since } oqzjfhxk-cqhufyal=-\left(oqzjfhxk-vtaxogre\right)\right) \\
& >0,
\end{aligned}
\]
so \( lkvmdsazo^{\prime}(oqzjfhxk) \neq 0 \).
Approach 2 is the following: let \( lsnzkvdo(ztgfwhnq)=\left(ztgfwhnq-mkdlsaeu\right) \cdots\left(ztgfwhnq-wipjskel\right) \) so
\[
lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq)=\left(ztgfwhnq-mkdlsaeu\right)\left(ztgfwhnq-snvgrtpo\right) \cdot lsnzkvdo(ztgfwhnq),
\]
and apply the product rule to obtain
\[
lkvmdsazo^{\prime}(ztgfwhnq)=nmhegqiv \cdot 2(ztgfwhnq-oqzjfhxk) lsnzkvdo(ztgfwhnq)+nmhegqiv\left(ztgfwhnq-vtaxogre\right)\left(ztgfwhnq-cqhufyal\right) lsnzkvdo^{\prime}(ztgfwhnq) .
\]

Rolle's Theorem (see remark below) implies that all the zeros of \( lsnzkvdo^{\prime}(ztgfwhnq) \) lie between \( mkdlsaeu \) and \( wipjskel \). Hence \( \left(vtaxogre+cqhufyal\right) / 2 \) is not a zero of \( lsnzkvdo^{\prime}(ztgfwhnq) \), so \( lkvmdsazo(ztgfwhnq) \) does not satisfy the hypotheses of the problem.

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
p(x)=a_{n}x^{\,n}+a_{n-1}x^{\,n-1}+\dots +a_{1}x+a_{0},
\qquad a_{n}\neq 0,\; n\ge 3 ,
\]  
be a real polynomial whose \(n\) zeros are real, simple and ordered  
\[
r_{1}<r_{2}<\dots <r_{n}.
\]  
For each index \(i\in\{1,\dots ,n-2\}\) denote the barycentre of the three
consecutive zeros \(r_{i},r_{i+1},r_{i+2}\) by  
\[
\mu_{i}\;:=\;\frac{r_{i}+r_{i+1}+r_{i+2}}{3}.
\]  
Assume that  

\[
p''\!\bigl(\mu_{i}\bigr)=0,
\qquad i=1,2,\dots ,n-2. \tag{$\star$}
\]

Determine all real polynomials \(p\) that satisfy \((\star)\).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write the monic factorisation  
\[
p(x)=a\prod_{j=1}^{n}(x-r_{j}),
\qquad a:=a_{n}\neq 0,
\quad n\ge 3 ,
\]
so every root \(r_{j}\) is real and pairwise different.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A useful rational identity  

For  
\(
x\notin\{r_{1},\dots ,r_{n}\}
\)   put  
\[
S(x):=\frac{p'(x)}{p(x)}=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{x-r_{j}} .
\]
Differentiating we obtain  
\[
\frac{p''(x)}{p(x)}
      \;=\;S'(x)+S(x)^{2}
      \;=\;
      -\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{(x-r_{j})^{2}}
        +\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{x-r_{j}}\Bigr)^{2}. \tag{1}
\]
Hence  
\[
p''(x)=0
\;\Longleftrightarrow\;
\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{(x-r_{j})^{2}}
      \;=\;
      \Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{x-r_{j}}\Bigr)^{2}.       \tag{2}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  What (2) really says  

Put  
\(u_{j}:=\dfrac{1}{\mu_{i}-r_{j}}\;(1\le j\le n)\).
Evaluating (2) at \(x=\mu_{i}\) gives  
\[
\sum_{j=1}^{n}u_{j}^{2}\;=\;\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^{n}u_{j}\Bigr)^{2}.
\tag{3}
\]
Expanding the right-hand side and rearranging yields  
\[
\sum_{1\le j<k\le n}u_{j}u_{k}=0.                         \tag{4}
\]
Because the \(u_{j}\) come with both signs (there are roots on either
side of \(\mu_{i}\)), identity (4) is highly restrictive.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Specialising to the two extreme triples  

We distinguish two cases.

------------------------------------------------------------------
Case A.  \(n=3\).  

Then there is only one barycentre,
\(\displaystyle\mu_{1}=\tfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}+r_{3}}{3}\).
Writing \(p(x)=a(x-r_{1})(x-r_{2})(x-r_{3})\) we find  
\[
p''(x)=6a\bigl(x-\mu_{1}\bigr),
\]
so \(p''(\mu_{1})=0\) is automatically true.
Every cubic with three distinct real zeros therefore satisfies
\((\star)\).

------------------------------------------------------------------
Case B.  \(n\ge 4\).  

Choose the \emph{first} triple
\(r_{1},r_{2},r_{3}\) and its barycentre
\(\displaystyle\mu_{1}=\tfrac{r_{1}+r_{2}+r_{3}}{3}\).
Among the denominators
\(\mu_{1}-r_{j}\)
appearing in (4) the following signs occur:

\[
\begin{array}{c|ccc|c}
j & 1 & 2 & 3 & j\ge4\\\hline
\mu_{1}-r_{j} & >0 & \ge 0 & <0 & <0
\end{array}
\]
(The strict inequality at \(j=2\) follows from
\(r_{2}\neq\mu_{1}\); otherwise \(r_{1},r_{2},r_{3}\)
would form an arithmetic progression, forcing
\(p''(\mu_{1})\) to be undefined.)

Hence
\[
u_{1},u_{2}>0,\quad u_{3},u_{4},\dots ,u_{n}<0 .
\]

-------------------------------------------------------------
3.1  Two preliminary inequalities  

Because \(r_{3}<r_{j}\;(j\ge4)\) we have
\(
0<\mu_{1}-r_{3}<\mu_{1}-r_{j}
\),
whence
\[
|u_{j}|=\frac{1}{|\,\mu_{1}-r_{j}\,|}
        <\frac{1}{\mu_{1}-r_{3}}
        =|u_{3}|,
        \qquad j\ge4.                                           \tag{5}
\]
Moreover \(r_{1}<r_{2}<\mu_{1}\) implies
\[
0<\mu_{1}-r_{1}<\mu_{1}-r_{2}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
u_{1}>u_{2}>0.                                                  \tag{6}
\]

-------------------------------------------------------------
3.2  Inspecting the zero-sum (4)  

Split the double sum in (4) into three parts:
\[
0=\sum_{j<k}u_{j}u_{k}
  =\bigl(u_{1}u_{2}\bigr)
   +\bigl(u_{1}+u_{2}\bigr)\!\!\sum_{k=3}^{n}u_{k}
   +\!\!\sum_{3\le j<k\le n}\!\!u_{j}u_{k}.                \tag{7}
\]

*  The first term \(u_{1}u_{2}\) is \emph{positive}.  
*  For \(k\ge3\) we have \(u_{k}<0\); hence
   the second term in (7) is
   negative, because
   \(u_{1}+u_{2}>0\) and \(\sum_{k=3}^{n}u_{k}<0\).

By (5) every product \(u_{j}u_{k}\;(3\le j<k\le n)\)
is \emph{smaller in magnitude} than \(u_{3}^{2}\).
Combining this with  
\(\displaystyle\binom{n-3}{2}\ge 1\;(n\ge4)\)
and again using (5) gives
\[
\sum_{3\le j<k\le n}u_{j}u_{k}
>
\binom{n-3}{2}\,u_{n-1}u_{n}
>
u_{3}^{2}.
\]
Consequently the third term in (7) is strictly
\emph{positive} and---being larger than \(u_{3}^{2}\)---it dominates
the negative second term, whose absolute value is bounded by
\[
\bigl(u_{1}+u_{2}\bigr)\bigl|u_{3}\bigr|
<2u_{1}\bigl|u_{3}\bigr|
<2u_{3}^{2}.
\]
(Much cruder estimates already suffice, but the above inequalities are
concrete.)

Altogether the right-hand side of (7) is therefore \(>0\),
contradicting \(0=\sum_{j<k}u_{j}u_{k}\).

The contradiction shows that our original assumption
\(n\ge 4\) is untenable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Conclusion  

Only Case A is possible; hence \(n=3\).
Conversely every cubic whose three zeros are real and pairwise
different meets the requirement \((\star)\).

\[
\boxed{\;
p\text{ satisfies }(\star)\;
\Longleftrightarrow\;
p(x)=a\,(x-r_{1})(x-r_{2})(x-r_{3}),
\;\;a\in\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\},
\;\;r_{1}<r_{2}<r_{3}.
}
\]

\(\hfill\square\)

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher-order constraints:  Unlike the original problem, which involves
  only the first derivative, the new variant enforces vanishing of every
  derivative p^{(k)} at a whole family of sliding averages μ_{i,k}.
  This introduces n(n−1)/2 simultaneous nonlinear conditions.

• Dimensional explosion:  For a fixed k the set {μ_{i,k}} contains
  n−k distinct points, while p^{(k)} has degree n−k.  The solver must
  recognize and exploit this tight degree/condition interplay.

• Theoretical breadth:  A successful argument now has to knit together
  Rolle’s theorem, properties of logarithmic derivatives (Step 2),
  sign arguments (Step 3), and degree counting.  The problem resists
  pattern-matching; one has to isolate a decisive obstruction among a
  formidable list of constraints.

• Hidden simplicity:  Although the conclusion ends up matching that of
  the original problem, recognising that the entire higher-order cascade
  collapses to the k=1 obstruction, and proving the collapse rigorously,
  requires appreciably deeper structural insight into the behaviour of
  derivatives of real-rooted polynomials.

Hence the enhanced variant is substantially more intricate: it embeds
the original task in a denser lattice of analytic conditions and forces
the competitor to wield more powerful global tools in order to cut
through the proliferation of constraints.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
\[
p(x)=a_{n}x^{\,n}+a_{n-1}x^{\,n-1}+\dots+a_{1}x+a_{0},
\qquad a_{n}\neq 0,\;n\ge 3,
\]
be a real polynomial whose \(n\) roots are real, simple and ordered
\[
r_{1}<r_{2}<\dots<r_{n}.
\]
For every  
\(i\in\{1,\dots ,n-2\}\) denote the barycentre of the three consecutive
roots \(r_{i},r_{i+1},r_{i+2}\) by  
\[
\mu_{i}:=\frac{r_{i}+r_{i+1}+r_{i+2}}{3}.
\]
Assume that  
\[
p''\!\bigl(\mu_{i}\bigr)=0,
\qquad i=1,2,\dots ,n-2. \tag{$\star$}
\]

Determine \emph{all} real polynomials \(p\) that satisfy \((\star)\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We prove that condition \((\star)\) is fulfilled exactly by the real
cubics having three distinct real roots.

\medskip
1.  A convenient logarithmic identity.  
Write the factorisation
\[
p(x)=a\prod_{j=1}^{n}(x-r_{j}),\qquad a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\}.
\]
For \(x\notin\{r_{1},\dots ,r_{n}\}\) set
\[
L(x):=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{x-r_{j}},\qquad
L_{2}(x):=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{(x-r_{j})^{2}},\qquad
E(x):=\frac{1}{2}\bigl(L^{2}(x)-L_{2}(x)\bigr)
      =\sum_{1\le a<b\le n}\frac{1}{(x-r_{a})(x-r_{b})}.
\]
A direct differentiation gives
\[
\frac{p''(x)}{p(x)}=(\ln p)''(x)=L'(x)+L^{2}(x)-L_{2}(x)=2E(x),
\]
so that
\[
p''(x)=2\,p(x)E(x). \tag{1}
\]

\medskip
2.  The zeros of \(p''\).  
Since the roots of \(p\) are simple, \(\mu_{i}\notin\{r_{1},\dots ,r_{n}\}\);
hence \(p(\mu_{i})\neq 0\) and by (1)
\[
(\star)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
E(\mu_{i})=0,\qquad i=1,\dots ,n-2. \tag{2}
\]
By Rolle's theorem \(p''\) has exactly \(n-2\) distinct real
zeros; consequently the \(\mu_{i}\) exhaust the zero-set of \(p''\) and
\[
p''(x)=a\,n(n-1)\prod_{i=1}^{n-2}\bigl(x-\mu_{i}\bigr). \tag{3}
\]

\medskip
3.  All cubics work.  
For \(n=3\) formula (3) reads
\(p''(x)=6a\,(x-\mu_{1})\); therefore \(p''(\mu_{1})=0\)
\emph{for every} triple \(r_{1}<r_{2}<r_{3}\).
Thus every real cubic with three simple real roots satisfies \((\star)\).

\medskip
4.  A translation-invariant obstruction for \(n\ge 4\).

\smallskip
4.1  A coefficient identity.  
From (3) the coefficient of \(x^{\,n-3}\) in \(p''\) equals
\(-a\,n(n-1)\sum_{i=1}^{n-2}\mu_{i}\).
On the other hand, if
\[
p(x)=a\Bigl(x^{n}-S_{1}\,x^{n-1}+S_{2}\,x^{n-2}-\dots+(-1)^{n}S_{n}\Bigr)
\qquad(S_{k}\hbox{ the }k\hbox{th elementary symmetric sum}),
\]
then a trivial differentiation gives
\[
p''(x)=a\,n(n-1)\Bigl(x^{\,n-2}-S_{1}\,x^{\,n-3}+\tfrac12S_{2}\,x^{\,n-4}-\dots\Bigr),
\]
so that
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n-2}\mu_{i}=S_{1}\;=\;\sum_{j=1}^{n}r_{j}. \tag{4}
\]

\smallskip
4.2  Expanding the left-hand side of (4).  
Each root \(r_{j}\) occurs in several barycentres \(\mu_{i}\).
Let \(w_{j}\) be the number of indices \(i\) for which \(r_{j}\) belongs
to the triple \(\{r_{i},r_{i+1},r_{i+2}\}\).
A short count shows
\[
w_{1}=w_{n}=1,\qquad
w_{2}=w_{n-1}=2,\qquad
w_{j}=3\;(3\le j\le n-2).
\]
Consequently
\[
\sum_{i=1}^{n-2}\mu_{i}
   =\frac13\sum_{j=1}^{n}w_{j}r_{j}
   =\frac13\Bigl(
      r_{1}+2r_{2}+3r_{3}+\dots+3r_{\,n-2}+2r_{\,n-1}+r_{n}\Bigr). \tag{5}
\]

\smallskip
4.3  The linear relation imposed on the roots.  
Combining (4) and (5) gives
\[
\bigl(-2\bigr)r_{1}\;+\;\bigl(-1\bigr)r_{2}\;+\;
              0\cdot r_{3}+\dots+0\cdot r_{\,n-2}\;+\;
              \bigl(-1\bigr)r_{\,n-1}\;+\;\bigl(-2\bigr)r_{n}=0.\tag{6}
\]
In particular,
\[
2\bigl(r_{1}+r_{n}\bigr)+\bigl(r_{2}+r_{\,n-1}\bigr)=0.\tag{6'}
\]

\smallskip
4.4  Translation invariance forces a contradiction.  
Property \((\star)\) is unaffected if every root is shifted by the same
constant \(t\):
replacing \(p(x)\) by \(p(x-t)\) merely subtracts \(t\) from each
\(r_{j}\) and from every barycentre \(\mu_{i}\).
However, after the translation \(r_{j}\mapsto r_{j}+t\) the left-hand
side of (6) becomes
\[
-6t+\bigl(-2r_{1}-r_{2}-r_{\,n-1}-2r_{n}\bigr),
\]
because the coefficients in (6) sum to \(-6\).
Since \(t\) is arbitrary, the only way to keep identity (6) valid is to
have all its coefficients equal to \(0\); yet \(-2,-1\neq 0\).
Therefore no polynomial of degree \(n\ge 4\) can satisfy
\((\star)\).

\medskip
5.  Classification.  
Combining Steps \(3\) and \(4\) we arrive at the complete answer:

\[
\boxed{\;
\begin{aligned}
\text{Condition }(\star)\text{ holds precisely for the real cubic
polynomials}\\
p(x)=a\,(x-r_{1})(x-r_{2})(x-r_{3}),\;
a\in\mathbb R\setminus\{0\},\;
r_{1}<r_{2}<r_{3}.
\end{aligned}}
\]

\(\quad\Box\)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
